summary:
Denmark's Abolition of the Slave Trade (March 16, 1792 AD) made Denmark the first country in the world to formally abolish the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] through legislation — preceding Britain's abolition by 15 years. The Danish ordinance, signed by Crown Prince Frederick (later Frederick VI), prohibited Danish participation in the slave trade to Danish colonies effective from January 1, 1803 — a decade's delay intended to allow Danish Caribbean plantation owners to 'complete their stock' of enslaved people before the trade ended. Denmark's Caribbean colonies (St. Croix, St. Thomas, and St. Jan, now the US Virgin Islands) had been developed through the slave trade, and their planters' economic interests created political resistance to immediate abolition.

The Danish abolition is often overlooked in histories of abolition because of the decade delay before the 1803 effective date and because Danish colonial slavery itself continued until 1848 (abolished by Governor-General Peter von Scholten under pressure from a slave uprising led by 'General Buddhoe'). The Danish abolition movement was inspired by Enlightenment humanitarianism and economic arguments (some believed free labor more profitable than slave labor) as well as humanitarian campaigns by figures like Paul Erdmann Isert. The British abolition of 1807, culminating decades of Quaker and Evangelical abolitionist campaigning led by William Wilberforce and the Society for Effecting the Abolition of the Slave Trade, was far more influential in ending the transatlantic slave trade globally because of Britain's dominant naval and commercial position. The abolition movements in Denmark, Britain, and France represented the beginning of a global process of slave trade suppression that would take the remainder of the century to complete.
